data.title: Circle Post-Quantum Roadmap: How to Change Locks in Advance for "Quantum Breakdown"
data.description:
Author: Karen Z, Foresight News

If quantum computers become powerful enough one day, blockchain will likely face two fundamental security assumptions: whether signatures can still prove "I am me," and whether data encrypted today will be decrypted in the future.

Circle's newly released post-quantum security roadmap paper discusses this very issue. Its core judgment is straightforward: elliptic curve cryptography, widely relied upon by blockchains today, including ECDSA, Ed25519, and BLS, will fail once faced with sufficiently powerful quantum computers. More problematic is that on EVM chains, accounts typically expose their public keys when broadcasting transactions for the first time; on chains like Bitcoin, addresses that have already spent, reused, or exposed their public keys in specific scripts will also enter a similar risk zone.

The paper's author lineup also indicates that this is not a typical popular science article.

The authors include Mira Belenkiy, Chief Software Engineer at Circle; Duc V. Le, Research Engineer at Circle; Gordon Liao, Chief Economist at Circle; Vipin Singh Sehrawat, Chief Security Engineer for Product Security at Circle; Dragos Rotaru, Research Engineer at Circle; and several Circle engineers, including Sergey Gorbunov, co-founder of Interop Labs (the original developer of the Axelar network) and now with Circle. Dan Boneh, a leading scholar in applied cryptography at Stanford University, also co-authored the paper. The most important aspect of this paper is not the fear-mongering narrative of "will quantum computing destroy cryptocurrencies?", but rather its breakdown of the problem into a real-world engineering migration issue. Circle argues that post-quantum migration is not a simple upgrade, but a "long-term relocation" across wallets, smart contracts, custody, cloud services, validators, and regulatory rules. The paper outlines several types of risks that blockchains face in the face of quantum attacks. The first type is account forgery. Once an address's public key is exposed, a future quantum attacker could potentially recover the private key and directly forge transactions. The paper cites Project Eleven's Bitcoin RisQ Metrics, stating that millions of addresses with balances are already exposed to quantum risks, including an estimated 14 million Bitcoin addresses. The second type is the "collect first, decrypt later" risk: attackers store encrypted data today and decrypt it later when quantum computing matures. The third type is consensus layer risk; if the validator's signing key is recovered, it could lead to double signatures, censorship, or even history rewriting. The fourth type is network layer risk; parts relying on traditional key exchange, such as P2P communication and RPC over TLS, also need upgrades. Circle's Three-Phase Migration Roadmap Circle's roadmap isn't simply about replacing one signature algorithm with another; it's divided into three steps: "Preparing Now," "Hybrid Transition," and "Final Switch." Each step has a different risk priority: privacy data must be protected first, accounts and smart contracts should be migrated gradually, and consensus and infrastructure should be switched over after the ecosystem, hardware, and standards are more mature. Attack Types and Countermeasures in the Arc Roadmap, Source: Circle Post-Quantum Security Roadmap Paper The first phase is the "Preparing Now" phase. The goal at this stage is not to immediately abolish ECDSA, but rather to provide a migration path for developers and users. Arc will support SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s post-quantum signature verification on the mainnet, allowing smart accounts to verify post-quantum signatures on-chain. In simpler terms, Arc is first installing an access control system on smart contracts that can recognize the new lock, but native transaction signatures will still retain ECDSA in the short term because post-quantum signatures are larger and slower to verify, impacting throughput and user experience. Meanwhile, Arc will support X-Wing HPKE encryption of transaction memos and protect transaction content, contract state, and execution traces through privacy execution. Circle places this part first because the privacy risk of "recorded today, decrypted in the future" is irreversible; signatures can be upgraded later, but leaked data cannot be restored to privacy. At the account layer, Circle also proposes several transition tools. For example, through EIP-4337 account abstraction, smart accounts can verify post-quantum signatures; through a hash-and-rotate scheme, only the public key hash is stored on-chain, minimizing the window of exposure of the public key plaintext; and through a post-quantum public key registry, users can bind their addresses and post-quantum public keys in advance. The common goal of these designs is to allow users to prepare for account migration without waiting for the underlying protocol to be completely transformed. The second phase is the "hybrid transition phase." This phase is the most realistic and also the most complex. USDC smart contracts will support both traditional signatures and post-quantum signatures simultaneously for a period of time. Once the ecosystem is ready, classic signatures will be disabled through a reservation mechanism. Circle also plans to migrate cold storage funds to multi-signature smart contracts to ensure compatibility with the migration schedules of different chains and different post-quantum signature algorithms. Since USDC smart contracts are deployed on more than 30 chains, it faces not a single-chain upgrade problem, but rather the fragmentation problem caused by the fragmentation resulting from each chain ecosystem choosing its own algorithm and setting its own timetable. The paper specifically emphasizes the challenges of ecrecover. Many EVM contracts use ecrecover to verify ECDSA signatures, but many of these contracts are no longer upgradable. Simply disabling ecrecover would break many existing applications; continuing to run it introduces the risk of quantum forgery. Circle proposes a promising solution: modifying ecrecover's behavior at the protocol layer through a hard fork, allowing it to support post-quantum signatures while maintaining the old ABI. This solution has significant practical implications because it doesn't just serve new contracts, but attempts to provide a migration path for existing, difficult-to-modify older contracts. The transition phase also includes updates to the underlying infrastructure. Circle needs to inventory its internal cryptography stack, assess whether cloud service providers, HSM, KMS, TEE, libp2p, TLS, and other dependencies have post-quantum preparedness capabilities, and rotate keys in the correct order. The paper specifically warns that if key A protects key B, and key B protects key C, then A must be rotated first, then B, and finally C. If the order is incorrect, even with a post-quantum algorithm, previously intercepted encrypted material may be exposed in the future. The third stage is the "final switch." Circle will only perform a true hard switch once the ecosystem, regulations, hardware wallets, cloud service providers, and blockchain infrastructure are all ready. At that time, Arc and USDC smart contracts may refuse ECDSA signatures, and validator signatures will migrate to the post-quantum scheme. If some chains carrying USDC fail to meet sufficient post-quantum security requirements in the long term, Circle may even consider suspending some contract functions or withdrawing support to avoid exposing user assets to the risk of quantum forgery. What to do with old accounts is the most difficult problem. But the final switch also brings the most intractable problem: what happens to the assets in accounts that haven't been migrated? Circle's stance is that freezing insecure accounts is to prevent theft and should not automatically equate to asset confiscation. In other words, "stopping control of old signatures" and "denying the economic rights of asset holders" must be handled separately. Therefore, the paper places great importance on account recovery, including migration to Arc, recovery via mnemonic phrases and zero-knowledge proofs, recovery via TEE proofs, and, in limited cases, recovery via off-chain legal documents, custodian proofs, exchange proofs, or estate documents. This leads to a crucial policy issue in the paper: account recovery. With the advent of the quantum era, traditional signatures themselves can no longer prove ownership, and KYC may not be able to prove who owns an anonymous address. Circle believes that regulators need to clarify in advance: how users should be notified before the migration deadline, what evidence is sufficient to prove asset ownership, how long after freezing assets is considered unclaimed, and how rules regarding estates, sanctions, anti-money laundering, and court orders should apply. The paper estimates that the industry may have a 5 to 10-year window to formulate these rules. This paper also offers a sober assessment: overly rapid migration could bring greater risks. For example, if companies currently use HSMs to protect their private keys, and hastily export these keys to ordinary CPUs for signing in order to catch up with post-quantum signatures, they will be more vulnerable to traditional hacker attacks. Circle's stance is that post-quantum migration should be prepared for early, but current security should not be compromised for the sake of "appearing secure." In simpler terms, Circle isn't saying "quantum computers will break the blockchain tomorrow," but rather that financial infrastructure cannot wait until the locks are proven to be ineffective before changing them. Especially for stablecoins like USDC that operate across more than 30 chains, the real challenge isn't just choosing a new algorithm, but getting wallets, contracts, custodians, validators, cloud service providers, regulators, and users to migrate together. Quantum attacks haven't actually materialized yet, but the migration costs are already a reality.

data.title: When will the "long live the chip industry" rally in US stocks end?
data.description:
Author: Sun Cheng; Source: Barron's

In April, US stocks were immersed in a structural frenzy led by AI, with the Nasdaq surging 15.3% in a single month, the S&P 500 rising 10.4%, and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index recording its largest monthly gain since 2000. After May, the market script quickly changed, with the three major US stock indices fluctuating at high levels. Beneath the surface calm, undercurrents were turbulent: ARM's stock price soared 42.58% in a single week thanks to positive earnings reports, while storage leaders such as SanDisk and Seagate saw weekly gains of over 15%, while former AI "flag bearers" Nvidia and Microsoft saw their stock prices decline.

Is this the cooling of the AI ​​main theme, or a profound internal shift? Funds are flowing from GPUs and cloud giants to ARM architecture and storage chips, and the market seems to be trading on a new logic of "training computing power reaching its peak, with edge inference and storage taking over."

Does the Dow Jones' record high signal the start of a style rotation? Is ARM's surge the beginning of a value reassessment or the peak of a short-term bubble? Key Points: 1. The AI ​​boom isn't over, but it's entering a phase of refined differentiation: Funds continue to flow into the AI ​​sector, but have shifted from a broad-based rally to selective betting. The market is shifting from GPUs and cloud giants to ARM architecture and memory chips, with the trading logic changing to "training computing power peaking, edge inference and storage taking over." 2. Strong herding effect, but with a shift in the target of herding: Quantitative trading, end-of-day options, and leveraged ETFs have changed the market ecosystem, and market maker hedging activities have exacerbated both rises and falls. The herding effect hasn't subsided, but has shifted from Nvidia and other stocks to new hot sectors like ARM and SanDisk, leading to "unbelievable" price increases. 3. The surge in ARM's stock price is a typical short squeeze and is unsustainable: The combined effect of gamma squeeze and short squeeze led to a short-term surge in the stock price. This trend cannot last forever; once buying pressure is exhausted or sentiment wavers, the stock price may quickly reverse downwards, requiring extra caution. 4. The ARM architecture has a fundamental advantage in edge inference: As a RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computing) system, ARM's energy efficiency is nearly half that of x86; its IP licensing model supports customized heterogeneous designs; and its matrix operation optimization is more suitable for the Transformer model. A long-term inflection point in the industry has been formed, but short-term stock price fluctuations are severe. 5. NVIDIA's position in training is solid, but it faces challenges in inference: The CUDA ecosystem's moat is difficult to overcome, and there are no substantial challengers in the training field. However, on the inference side, AMD and its self-developed chips from Google and Microsoft have gained preferential treatment due to the long-standing dominance of Nvidia. While Nvidia's market share will decrease in the future, it will still remain the dominant player. 6. Inference computing power load has exceeded training, and will account for 70-80% in the future: Currently, the ratio of inference to training computing power load is about 6:4, but capital expenditure still accounts for 60% of training. With the popularization of large models and the development of Agentic AI, coupled with the slowdown in training iterations, the stringent requirements for cost, power consumption, and latency on the inference side bring new opportunities for ARM and memory chips. Market Review: Is the AI-led Frenzy the Beginning or a Bubble? Sun Cheng: Has the AI-led Rise Ended? What is the core driving force behind the flow of funds from GPU/cloud giants to ARM and memory? Cat Sister: Since 2023, the US stock market has been focusing heavily on AI. The star stock of 2023 was Nvidia, a semiconductor company, but primarily driven by AI. This wave of growth continued into 2024 and 2025, and is now unfolding in a tiered and rhythmic manner across the board. Therefore, it's too early to say the AI ​​boom is over. Looking at our latest monitoring data from last Friday's US stock market, there are still no signs of a decrease in funds flowing into the AI ​​sector. However, there is a significant characteristic: "differentiation within differentiation." We've seen the overall US stock market stagnate since October of last year, trading sideways from October to February. During this sideways movement, some software stocks and some large-cap tech stocks impacted by AI have actually shown signs of exhaustion and correction. However, during the same period when the broader market was consolidating or even declining, some popular AI sectors, such as storage and optical communications, not only didn't fall but actually rose. This reflects market differentiation and the focus of capital. Therefore, from a capital monitoring perspective, both new and existing buyers remain very active. The market exhibits a continuous trend: within the broader AI sector, capital is constantly seeking hot spots, differentiation, new themes, and companies in different tracks. Therefore, the upward trend hasn't ended but has entered a more refined phase. Sun Cheng: The Nasdaq and S&P 500 achieved their best monthly performance in nearly six years, and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index recorded its largest monthly gain since 2000. Behind this extreme market performance, besides AI-driven factors, in what specific ways is market sentiment recovery and the herding effect of capital manifested? Since the market didn't continue its broad-based rise after mid-May, does this mean market sentiment has shifted from "overall optimism" to "selective betting"? Does the herding effect still exist? Cat Sister: This can be viewed from two perspectives. Firstly, it's a typical technical market movement in the US stock market. Generally speaking, after a prolonged period of sideways movement and decline, the US stock market experiences a retaliatory surge, such as the market in 2020, and the nearly six-month surge following a three-month correction from August to the end of October 2023. Starting last October, the US stock market stagnated, with its center of gravity remaining unchanged for several months – a typical consolidation phase. The final drop in March was a severe valuation correction, with March 30th becoming the bottom of this cycle. Therefore, the surge that began in April was essentially a correction of the previous nearly six months of sideways movement. A significant characteristic of the US stock market is "stay in the game" – you must remain involved, because a rebound can be completed in just one or two days. Statistics show that, regardless of the past 50, 30, or 10 years, missing these few single-day or multi-day rebounds and surges throughout the year results in significantly different annual returns. On the other hand, the herding effect of funds still exists, but the market today is completely different from before. Quantitative trading, machine-programmed trading, the large participation of retail investors, and the massive amount of options and leveraged ETFs have fundamentally changed the market ecosystem. Market makers, in order to hedge risks, have to take certain actions, which exacerbate market rises and falls. Especially in the leading AI hot stocks, all retail investors are trading end-of-day options, while large investors (players with large capital and market influence) are also chasing these hot stocks. This has caused accelerated rises and falls, leading to the increasingly strong herding effect in US stocks in recent years, with more and more phenomena of "rising to the point of being mind-blowing". Therefore, the market has not shifted from optimism to pessimism, but rather the objects of herding have changed. Funds are still concentrated, but the targets have switched. Sun Cheng: ARM's weekly increase exceeded 42%, achieving such a huge increase in just two trading days. Technically, is this a "short squeeze"? Is there any boost from retail investor sentiment or option gamma squeeze? Is this rate of increase sustainable? Cat Sister: This is indeed a very typical short squeeze, with the gamma squeeze effect playing a significant role. In the current US stock market, once a hot topic emerges, all institutions, large investors, and retail investors chase it. The existence of a large number of expiry options creates a gamma squeeze problem. Many market makers don't bet on the direction, but rather profit from the spread between buying and selling. When most players in the market buy expiry options or near-term out-of-the-money call options, market makers face huge risks: once the stock price rises, they need to cash out their options, and to hedge the risk (maintain delta neutrality), they have to continuously buy a large amount of the underlying stock. The more people buy, the more underlying stock the market maker needs to buy, thus driving the stock price up wildly. Many of the recent US stock market closing 30-minute effects we've seen are caused by this squeeze effect. Looking back, seeing the stock price being pushed up again and again, excited investors will continue to increase their positions by buying call options, which further forces market makers to buy underlying stock to hedge. In addition, there is the short squeeze. Some investors believe the stock price is significantly deviating from its valuation and choose to short sell, but due to the aforementioned mechanism, the stock price continues to rise, ultimately forcing short sellers to liquidate their positions and buy shares, resulting in a short squeeze. In the case of ARM stock, if this trend lasts longer and the curve is steeper, it will become a typical short squeeze. Regarding sustainability, this kind of market movement is technically unsustainable indefinitely. Once all the short sellers have surrendered, or new buyers' confidence is slightly shaken by the stock price fluctuations, the market loses its controversy and buffer, and the stock price is prone to quickly reversing downwards when profit-taking occurs or market sentiment hesitates slightly, and like the rise, it also has a cumulative effect. Therefore, this kind of market movement should be approached with extreme caution. In-depth analysis of AI technology: ARM's rise, Nvidia's pressure, and storage taking the lead. Sun Cheng: ARM surged 42.58% last week. The core trading logic of the market is that "AI is shifting from training to edge inference, and the ARM architecture will benefit significantly." From a technical perspective, what are the essential advantages of ARM's instruction set and licensing model compared to x86 in AI inference scenarios? Is this round of value reassessment a short-term financial report catalyst or a long-term industry turning point? Wang Huai: From a technical perspective, the market is now shifting towards AI computing power applications mainly based on edge inference, and this major logic is quite obvious. There are three core reasons why ARM is more suitable for edge computing than x86: First, instruction set advantage. ARM is RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computing), while x86 is CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computing). RISC architecture doesn't require overly complex pipeline designs, allowing for the integration of more cores, caches, or dedicated acceleration units within the same chip area, which is beneficial for near-memory computing. In AI inference, a major bottleneck is memory communication. ARM's flexible physical IP and customization capabilities make it naturally suited for AI inference operations. Under typical edge loads, the ARM architecture is significantly more energy efficient than x86, typically consuming nearly half the power, resulting in a significant cost advantage. Secondly, there's the advantage of its licensing model. ARM uses an IP licensing model, allowing customers to customize their designs after obtaining a license, such as how the CPU works with the GPU and NPU. x86, on the other hand, is a "black box," making it difficult to perform heterogeneous layered optimization designs. This openness makes ARM more popular when designing high-bandwidth memory (LPDDR) integrations. Thirdly, there's matrix operation optimization. Matrix multiplication, the most frequently used operation in Transformer models, is much more efficient on ARM than on x86. While x86 can also perform matrix multiplication efficiently, it's less energy-efficient and area-efficient than ARM's vector solutions for achieving the same throughput, making it less cost-effective on edge devices. If compatibility and maturity are your concerns, x86 is a good choice; however, if cost-effectiveness and power consumption (a significant future cost option) are paramount, ARM has a clear advantage. From an industry trend perspective, AI is shifting from training to inference, especially towards Agentic AI. This involves not only large-scale model dialogues but also numerous API calls, network and file accesses, all of which rely on the CPU. ARM, as a CPU, holds great promise for Agentic AI applications on edge devices (PCs, mobile phones, automobiles, etc.). I believe the general direction is correct, and the industry inflection point has been recognized. However, short-term stock price fluctuations will be significant, but in the long run, the implementation of these major trends will solidify value. The long term is a weighing machine, the short term a sentiment voter. Sun Cheng: Nvidia fell 2.58% last week, with the market concerned about its slowing growth. AMD rose 3.60%. In the AI ​​chip field, is Nvidia's CUDA ecosystem still a strong moat? Can AMD's MI series GPUs narrow the gap in inference scenarios? How significant is the threat posed by customers' self-developed chips to Nvidia? Wang Huai: On the training side, no one can currently shake Nvidia's position. However, with the popularization of large models and the development of Agentic AI, the computing power demand for inference has increased significantly, giving other players an opportunity. The requirements for inference are more complex, requiring collaborative design of heterogeneous architectures such as CPUs, GPUs, and NPUs, and also having high memory requirements. This gives manufacturers like AMD a real opportunity to get a share of the market. AMD will definitely benefit from the sentiment that "the world has suffered under Nvidia's dominance for too long" in the inference field. Enterprises need a reliable second supplier to ensure supply chain security and avoid being held hostage by a single supplier. Therefore, as long as a viable second supplier emerges, customers will give them extra support. AMD's data center business is growing significantly, but overall revenue growth is still far lower than its stock price increase. While emotional fluctuations certainly play a role, the market's recognition of the huge opportunities in inference is real. For Nvidia, inference sales will be affected to some extent, but not fatally. CUDA's competitive advantage cannot be easily surpassed overnight. Meanwhile, Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and others are all developing their own inference chips, partly to reduce reliance on a single supplier and partly to create customized heterogeneous designs for their specific strengths. This trend is clear. However, Nvidia remains the most versatile and widely used chip provider. In the next two to three years, even in inference, Nvidia will still hold a major share, although the concentration will not be as high as in training. There are no substantial challengers in the training sector in the short term. Sun Cheng: Market opinion suggests that AI investment is shifting from "training computing power" to "inference applications." In terms of actual deployment, what is the current ratio of training to inference in global AI workloads? How will the inference share change in the next two years? What new technical challenges does this shift pose to chip design, storage bandwidth, and power consumption? Wang Huai: I've reviewed some reports, and currently the computing power load ratio for inference and training is approximately 6:4 or 5.5:4.5, with inference already exceeding training. However, capital expenditure (Capex) is reversed, with training still accounting for 60% and inference for 40%, because the cost of supernodes required for training is higher. The proportion of inference will increase significantly in the future. On the one hand, the global adoption rate of large-scale model applications is still very low. Data shows that 86% of the world's population has never used large-scale model applications. Apart from China and the US, many countries have very few large-scale model applications. On the other hand, Agentic AI will significantly increase the number of times large-scale models are called upon. In the next three to five years, it is entirely possible for inference computing power to reach 70% to 80%. Another reason is that the iteration speed on the training side is slowing down significantly. Companies capable of training large-scale models are consolidating, and fewer and fewer companies can afford the training costs. With reduced competition, companies will be more inclined to commercialize existing models rather than rapidly launching new ones. The computational power requirements for training will relatively decrease. Unless a new breakthrough in 10,000-calorie-level training occurs in areas such as embodied intelligence or world models, that could become a new variable. This shift from training to inference places higher demands on chip design. Inference is extremely sensitive to cost, power consumption, and latency, and needs to adapt to diverse scenarios from cloud to edge, providing segmented entry opportunities for different architectures (such as GPUs, ASICs, and CPUs). The requirements for memory bandwidth and key-value cache (KV Cache) are also more complex than for training. These technical challenges have precisely given architectures like ARM and memory chips more market opportunities.

data.title: Forgot to set limits: One company burned through $500 million in Claude in one month.
data.description:
Author: Bao Yilong; Source: Wall Street News

The enterprise AI boom is encountering its first real billing crisis.

On May 28, according to Axios, citing an AI consultant, one of its enterprise clients recently spent $500 million on Claude in a single month simply because no cap was set on employee usage.

Analysts believe that many companies, in rapidly deploying AI tools, focus on functionality and promotion, neglecting the establishment of cost control mechanisms.

Tech giants such as Microsoft and Amazon are taking action to cut internal AI tools or halt projects tracking AI usage in order to curb excessive consumption behavior known as "tokenmaxxing."

An Amazon senior vice president had to warn employees: "Please don't use AI for the sake of using AI." The core issue facing the market now is no longer "whether to embrace AI," but rather "what have we gained from burning so much money?" Amazon's decision to shut down its ranking system reveals the real costs of internal "score manipulation." Amazon's case exposes the dilemma of corporate AI governance from another perspective. According to reports citing two sources familiar with the matter, Amazon's developer platform Kiro once had an internal ranking system called "Kirorank," which scored employees based on their AI usage activity. However, the ranking unexpectedly led to employees having AI agents perform meaningless tasks to improve their rankings, directly causing an increase in the company's computing power consumption. Amazon Senior Vice President Dave Treadwell admitted to employees this week that while the ranking's intentions were good, the end result was that employees were driving up the company's operating costs through "token maxing." He explicitly instructed employees not to focus on token consumption but to concentrate on building better products, emphasizing "don't use AI for the sake of using AI." Amazon subsequently confirmed in a statement that the beta dashboard "was not an official or approved tool and has been taken offline." A similar situation occurred with Meta, where employees also attempted to gain a favorable position in the company's internal rankings by increasing token consumption. This phenomenon suggests that when companies incorporate AI usage into their performance evaluations, it can backfire, distorting employee incentives into ineffective consumption of computing power. Amazon subsequently shifted to replacing token consumption with a "normalized deployment" metric, focusing on tracking whether engineers can consistently generate valuable code through AI. It's worth noting that Amazon's capital expenditure this year is projected to reach $200 billion, with the vast majority going towards AI and data center infrastructure. Four Major Challenges: Why AI Investment Isn't Yielding Returns According to Axios, enterprise AI adoption is facing four structural obstacles. Misaligned Use Case Selection. Sophia Velastegui, CEO of Velastegui Ventures and former Chief AI Officer at Microsoft, stated that most people tend to use AI to automate tasks they dislike, rather than the tasks most valuable to the company. She believes that companies should focus their AI resources on scenarios that directly drive revenue, rather than blindly deploying them. Lack of cost control. AI queries are not without cost; enterprise-level packages are charged per token, and even simple daily queries can quickly accumulate into considerable expenses, which most business departments are unaware of. Human resources are the biggest bottleneck. Velastegui characterizes the current common practice of companies "scattering" AI licensing as a path that cannot bring substantial returns. Companies pile a large number of AI tools on employees, but lack effective guidance and focus, resulting in low actual adoption efficiency. Concerns exist regarding data sharing. Josh Pantony, CEO of Boosted.ai, a company focusing on AI tools for the financial industry, points out that when companies are unwilling to open up their proprietary internal data to AI agents due to concerns about data security, the actual effectiveness of the agents will be greatly reduced, and the return on investment will naturally be out of the question. Token Economics: A New Core Variable in the AI ​​Narrative Behind this debate lies a more complex investment logic being reconstructed. According to Wall Street Insights, Rich Privorotsky, head of Goldman Sachs' One-Delta division, has recently stated that the core variable in AI trading has shifted from "whether the technology is feasible" to "whether the cost is affordable." DeepSeek reportedly lowered its token pricing by 75%, and Xiaomi MiMo's price reduction was close to 99%. This cost compression may trigger a "price war" logic similar to that following subsidy competition. He pointed out that infrastructure bottlenecks will eventually ease, and the market should not pay an excessive premium for "problems that are about to be solved." Rich Privorotsky further hypothesized that cheaper tokens might be the first to replace high-cost inference services. If there is a time lag in demand expansion, cloud service providers, model companies, and AI infrastructure revenue growth may face temporary pressure. He believes that the rationalization of token spending may become an important issue at the board level in the second and third quarters of this year, no less important than the AI ​​growth narrative itself. According to the Bloomberg Silicon Data LLM Token Expenditure Index, token prices have risen by about 65% since the end of February this year, while US AI software prices have increased by 20% to 37% cumulatively over the past year. This cost trend is prompting companies to re-examine their AI procurement strategies. As achieving 90% output at 10% cost becomes increasingly feasible, companies' reliance on high-cost cutting-edge models may systematically decrease. Ali Ansari, CEO of AI model training company Micro1, stated that companies are experiencing a "healthy swing" from overuse of AI to rational use. He believes that: Currently, the only truly effective area for AI is programming. The debate between bulls and bears: the same reality, two interpretations. Regarding the return on AI investment, the same data, analyzed under different frameworks, points to drastically different conclusions. From a bullish perspective, the current chaos is merely a normal growing pain in the transformation process. According to Goldman Sachs' Jim Schneider's assessment in early May, by 2030, agent-based AI will drive a 24-fold increase in token consumption, and gross margins for hyperscale cloud service providers and model providers will turn positive within the next 3 to 12 months. JPMorgan Chase's economic research also found that Python packages saw a jump in PyPI in early 2026, a trend that did not occur when ChatGPT launched in 2022, indicating that real productivity improvements are underway. A bearish perspective was systematically elaborated by Goldman Sachs semiconductor analyst Jim Covello in a report in April. He pointed out that almost all the value in the AI ​​supply chain has flowed to semiconductor companies, which is unprecedented and unsustainable in history. Chip companies should benefit when their customers benefit, but in this cycle, their prosperity has come at the expense of the entire upstream of the industry chain. Both narratives are unfolding simultaneously, and the outcome remains unclear. What is certain is that the simplistic equation of "increased token consumption equals successful AI transformation" has been broken. From extreme cases of burning through $500 million in a single month to Amazon's halt to ranking manipulation, AI investments are undergoing stricter scrutiny regarding their returns. The true verdict in this high-stakes gamble will be determined by how much real value the next AI bill generates.
